summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Patient reports fatigue and back pain related to her breasts.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports fatigue for the past two months, attributing it to her breasts when working at her desk all day. She has been considering a breast reduction for about six or seven years and reports experiencing back pain and pain in her neck and upper back. The patient reports that her bra strap indentations are present in her shoulders. She wears a 34e sports bra and reports getting rashes underneath her breasts. The patient feels like her activities are limited when holding things, such as working out or jumping or running. The patient's husband has accepted a new job. The patient reports being generally healthy. She has lost about 10 pounds over the past few months and wants to lose about 20 more pounds. She drinks a little wine on occasion. The patient is currently studying psychology. The patient has a history of breast cancer in her family; her mother had breast cancer at age 40. The patient was recommended to start mammograms at age 30. The patient does not have any children now and is planning to have children in the future.

Findings
The doctor measures the patient's breasts and notes that one breast is lower than the other. The doctor will remove tissue from the breast and elevate the nipple position. The doctor will make an incision around the nipple and an incision underneath the breast, using an anchor-like shape for the incision. The patient's breasts are asymmetrical and severely ptotic (drooping). There is no nipple retraction or discharge. There is no breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy. The doctor takes photos of the patient. The doctor's office will submit the request for approval. The patient is a good candidate for a breast reduction and will benefit from a breast reduction. The patient's minimum required resection is 1,792 grams. The schnur scale is 2.65. The patient may need a nipple graft. The patient's breasts are made up of fat and a gland, and the gland produces milk. The patient may not be able to breastfeed in the future. The hormones of pregnancy will change the breasts. The nipple sensation may be altered or absent after surgery. There is a slight chance of nipple sensation being absent. The patient may experience bruising after surgery, and the bruising will take some time to go away.

Assessment
The patient presents with bilateral breast ptosis and asymmetry, potentially contributing to her reported back and neck pain. A breast reduction is indicated to address these concerns.

Plan
The doctor will keep the patient overnight. The doctor will control the patient's pain and prevent the patient from being nauseated. The doctor will perform a breast reduction. The surgery will take about three hours. The patient will have drains in each side of the breast for about a week. The patient will have a clear plastic tape over the incision for about a week. The scars will take about a year to mature. The doctor will meet with the patient before surgery and will answer any last-minute questions. The doctor will provide detailed instructions.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Macromastia.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female who presents for evaluation of macromastia.

Ms. Jones reports this is her first visit in regard to a breast reduction and has been interested in getting a breast reduction for approximately 7 years. Currently she has Blue Cross Blue Shield insurance but will be added to her spouse’s insurance plan in 07/2021.

Current breast size is a G cup, although she primarily wears a 34 E sports bra for comfort, and desired breast size is a B or C cup. No prior pregnancies, potentially planning on pregnancies in the future, and unsure of breast feeding.

The patient endorses back and neck pain since she was in high school. Her back and neck pain has been managed by a chiropractor for a few years. She reports weight loss of 10 pounds over the past few months and would like to lose an additional 20 pounds.

She endorses the following symptoms because of her macromastia for 7 years:

Chronic upper back pain: Yes.
Bra strap grooving in shoulders or indentation on lateral chest wall: Occasionally.
Neck pain: Yes.
Nipple pain or numbness: Yes.
Paresthesia of the hands or arms: Yes.
Intertrigo, rash or yeast or other skin problems beneath breasts: Yes, persistent sweating.
Stooped posture: Yes.
Physical activity limited by breast size: Yes.

The patient has undergone the following therapies:
Physical therapy: No.

Special bras: Yes.
Non-narcotic pain medication: No.
Narcotic pain medication: No.

Her previous breast surgery includes:
Previous reconstruction: No.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Significant for migraine headaches.

FAMILY HISTORY

Mother with prior history of breast cancer at age 40.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Tobacco: No prior history.
Alcohol: Socially. One glass per week.
Drug: No prior history.
Occupational and Educational: Currently studying to be a psychologist.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Propranolol for migraine prevention.

VITALS

BSA 2.65

PHYSICAL EXAM

Breasts
Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally. There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally. No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.

ASSESSMENT

• Macromastia.

Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia presenting for evaluation of breast reduction. The patient has attempted non-surgical treatments with minimal relief and is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. Her minimum required resection based on a BSA of 2.65 with the Schnur scale is 1792 g, which should be attainable. Given her extremely large breasts and the measurements above, there is potential for using a free nipple graft.

PLAN

An extensive discussion of breast reduction surgery was performed with the patient with consideration of the patient's age, co-morbidities, previous surgical history, body habitus, BMI, and smoking history. Surgical time, immediate recovery course, and longer term recovery course were discussed. Relief of the patient's current symptoms related to macromastia was not guaranteed though is expected to improve. Specific breast size was discussed, also not guaranteed. I explained the expected outcomes of using a free nipple graft such as an insensate nipple and an inability to breast feed. The patient understands that the decision for a free nipple graft will be made intraoperatively based on assessment of nipple perfusion.

Risks and benefits of were discussed with the patient The patient understands the above risks, benefits and alternatives to surgery and wishes to proceed with bilateral reduction mammaplasty.

The patient will be switching to a different insurance policy in 07/2021. Once we get the new insurance information from the patient, we will submit it to her insurance company. If approved, she will return for preoperative counseling and consent.

Return to clinic prior to scheduled surgery date for preoperative counseling and consent.

The doctor will keep the patient overnight. The doctor will control the patient's pain and prevent the patient from being nauseated. The doctor will perform a breast reduction. The surgery will take about three hours. The patient will have drains in each side of the breast for about a week. The patient will have a clear plastic tape over the incision for about a week. The scars will take about a year to mature. The doctor will meet with the patient before surgery and will answer any last-minute questions. The doctor will provide detailed instructions.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor will remove tissue from the breast and elevate the nipple position. The doctor will make an incision around the nipple and an incision underneath the breast, using an anchor-like shape for the incision.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are planned surgical steps and operative approach, not current exam or diagnostic findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor's office will submit the request for approval.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Submitting a request for approval is an administrative/management action and belongs in the plan, not in exam findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient is a good candidate for a breast reduction and will benefit from a breast reduction.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a clinical judgment/diagnostic impression about candidacy and expected benefit, which is part of the assessment rather than objective findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's minimum required resection is 1,792 grams. The schnur scale is 2.65.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These quantitative criteria support the diagnostic impression and surgical indication; while somewhat exam-related, they more directly inform the assessment of surgical necessity.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient may need a nipple graft. The patient may not be able to breastfeed in the future. The hormones of pregnancy will change the breasts. The nipple sensation may be altered or absent after surgery. There is a slight chance of nipple sensation being absent. The patient may experience bruising after surgery, and the bruising will take some time to go away.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are counseling points and discussion of potential surgical risks and postoperative expectations, which belong in the plan rather than in objective findings.
